quote: Hey, Dave. This is Bart Drum with Ion. I was just calling because we had a request here you're looking at solar for the home. How you doing?
stewart_read: Bart opens with a generic 'we had a request' frame and skips any legitimacy establishment. No Tesla framing, no state mention, no 'certified installer' anchor. The schema intro template exists precisely to answer 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds — Bart's opener does none of that work. The call proceeds without resistance here, but that's Dave's disposition, not Bart's technique.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bart may have known this was not a Tesla-sourced lead and correctly used a non-Tesla intro. However, even the standard intro template requires a reference to the form submission for legitimacy — 'we had a request here' is vague enough to read as cold-call framing.
coaching_implication: Pull the lead source for this call. If it was a Tesla lead, Bart missed the schema intro entirely. If it was a form-fill lead, he still skipped the form-reference anchor ('the solar form you filled out online'). Either way, the opener did no legitimacy work. Manager should drill the intro variants with Bart until the legitimacy beat is automatic.

stewart_read: Dave says 'ComEdair' — not a recognized utility name. ComEd (Commonwealth Edison) serves northern Illinois; 'ComEdair' is likely a mispronunciation or mishearing. Bart accepts it without clarification ('Okay. Sweet.'). If the utility name is wrong, bill collection instructions, net metering context, and any utility-specific framing the closer uses will be built on a bad foundation.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bart simply didn't catch the anomaly. There is no evidence of a strategic reason to accept an unverified utility name.
coaching_implication: Bart should have reflected the name back: 'ComEd — is that Commonwealth Edison?' One clarifying question here protects the entire downstream bill-collection and closer-prep chain. Manager should use this as a drill moment: when a utility name sounds off, verify it before moving on.

quote: I think it's, like, 400 a month.
stewart_read: Bart captures the $400 figure and immediately moves to a soft savings frame ('cut that back at least, save you some money'). He does not flip the bill into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The schema pattern is capture + acknowledge + flip; Bart delivers capture + soft-acknowledge + move on. Dave has already told Bart he had a $16 summer bill on his other house — the flip was available and would have landed hard: 'You already know what solar does to a bill. Imagine what it does to $400 a month.' That line was available at 02:14 and was not used.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bart may have been conserving energy for the appointment pitch. However, there is no evidence of a later flip attempt — the bill is mentioned once and dropped.
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor coaching gap per the schema: every rep captures the bill and none flip it. Manager should run the objection_inversion drill with Bart specifically using the $400 figure and Dave's own $16 prior-solar reference as the raw material. The contrast was sitting right there.

quote: $16 summertime bill sounds really good with me. Yeah.
stewart_read: Dave volunteers a concrete, emotionally positive outcome from 26 years of prior solar ownership. This is the highest-quality enthusiasm signal in the call — a customer who already believes in the product, has lived the savings, and is motivated to replicate it. Bart responds with 'Awesome. I love it.' and moves on. He does not anchor on it, does not use it to deepen the interest question, and does not reference it again when the $400 bill comes up 70 seconds later.
coaching_implication: Bart had a gift here and walked past it. The $16 bill story is the emotional engine of this entire call — it's Dave's own proof point. Manager should show Bart how to anchor on it: 'That's exactly why you're calling — you already know what this does. Let's see if we can get you back there on this house.' Then tie it to the $400 figure when it surfaces. This is a two-beat sequence Bart missed entirely.

quote: You're probably looking at the garage.
stewart_read: Dave corrects Bart's satellite read and volunteers that the garage has a 5-year roof, no tree obstruction, and is the more suitable surface. He is doing Bart's site-qualification work for him — this is an engaged, knowledgeable customer who has already thought through the install. Bart responds adequately ('Yeah. For sure. It definitely looks more suitable') but does not use this moment to deepen Dave's ownership of the decision or build curiosity about what a garage-mounted system would produce.
coaching_implication: Dave's self-directed site analysis is a strong engagement signal. Manager should coach Bart to reflect it back as validation and use it to create curiosity: 'You've already scoped this out — that's exactly the kind of detail our specialist needs. Let's make sure Wednesday gives you the full picture on what that garage can do.' Turns Dave's knowledge into appointment motivation.

stewart_read: Dave states the house roof is approximately 20 years old. Per the schema, a roof over 15 years often means re-roof required before solar install — and Ion does roofing in-house. Bart does not flag this. Note: Bart's 'Perfect' at 05:36 responds to Dave's 5-year garage roof statement, not to the 20-year house roof. The 20-year house roof disclosure is accepted without any follow-up. This is a missed cross-sell signal and a potential DQ flag that the closer will need to navigate.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bart may have been deferring the roof-age issue to the specialist. However, there is no evidence he flagged it for the closer either — it does not appear in any handoff language.
coaching_implication: The 20-year house roof is both a DQ risk (schema dq_rules: roof_too_old_no_replacement) and a cross-sell opportunity (Ion roofing in-house). Bart should have asked: 'Has there been any talk of replacing that house roof?' If yes, bridge to roofing team. If no, flag for closer. Manager should drill the roof-age trigger with Bart — it's a two-second question that protects the sit and opens a revenue line.

stewart_read: Dave books Wednesday 11AM but explicitly qualifies it as 'tentative.' Bart accepts this without deploying any of the required button_up elements: no 'Is that fair?' verbal handshake, no one-hour block statement, no no-driving/at-computer requirement, no phone-appointment specification. The schema is explicit that skipping these elements raises no-show rate. Dave has already said he plays every day by ear — the appointment has no structural anchors holding it in place.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bart may have judged that pressing harder on a reluctant scheduler would lose the booking entirely. This is plausible — Dave showed three rounds of scheduling resistance. However, the button_up elements are not pressure tactics; they are expectation-setters. Skipping them doesn't reduce pressure, it reduces show rate.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Bart through the button_up checklist against this call. The 'Is that fair?' handshake in particular is a low-friction verbal commitment that Dave would likely have given — it costs nothing and converts 'tentatively' into a spoken agreement. Bart left all five button_up elements on the table.

quote: Do you have a program where you put a roof on too? Or
stewart_read: Dave asks directly about Ion's roofing program after the appointment is set. Bart confirms Ion has roofing programs but immediately disclaims knowledge ('I don't know, like, a ton of the details') and defers entirely to the specialist. The schema cross-sell template calls for a bridge: acknowledge the signal, name the capability, and route to the roofing team in real time. Bart's response is technically correct (don't fabricate details) but misses the bridge entirely — no enthusiasm, no routing action, no capture of Dave's roofing interest for the closer.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bart correctly avoided scope creep by not explaining roofing details. However, the schema distinguishes between explaining details (scope creep) and bridging to the right team (correct procedure). Bart did neither — he acknowledged and dropped.
coaching_implication: Manager should show Bart the cross-sell bridge template: 'Wait — you're asking about roofing? Ion actually does roofing in-house, both standalone and as part of a solar install. Let me flag that for our roofing team so they can reach out alongside the solar appointment.' This is a 15-second move that captures a second revenue line. Bart had the opening and left it on the table.
